text: The robotic system is utilized to perform a staple-less end colostomy reversal, involving precise manipulation of the colostomy site and surrounding tissues.

We present a case of a seven-year-old gentleman with end colostomy from ischemic colon post-elective ax-fem bypass graft, BMI of thirty-four, ASK score of three, former smoker. Extensive past medical history, as listed below, and a surgical history mostly related to his vascular disease. Cats can demonstrate the end colostomy and the flank hernia on the patient right with a bypass graft.
Four 8.5mm ports are placed in a straight line to access the left lower quadrant and pelvis for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure by performing lysis of adhesions, carefully detaching all attachments in the pelvis surrounding the colostomy.
The surgical team turns their attention to the pelvis, preparing the area for anastomosis by identifying the previously placed suture.

The surgeon identifies the peritoneal reflection and performs limited dissection to preserve blood supply. Using a bipolar energy device, the surgeon divides the attachments, scar tissue, and inflamed tissue, and then divides the colon distally and proximally with short bursts of energy.
The surgeon divides the descending colon distally, preparing for an end-to-end anastomosis without tension, following the patient's bowel prep.
The surgeon replaces the SASE suture using a 3-0 barbed suture. Two sutures are placed in the middle of the surgical site, starting at the back wall, and are run in opposite directions.

The surgical team switches to an ICG view to assess the blood supply during the anastomosis. Intravenous ICG is administered in two separate doses while the Cornell suture technique is applied in a running fashion.

The reason we chose this view in order to assess continuously the blood supply as this patient has suffered from ischemic colon in the past and has extensive history of peripheral vascular disease, coronary artery disease. so we thought it's very important to assess the blood supply while performing the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the anastomosis, meeting in the middle anteriorly, ensuring there are no gaps, and locks the sutures on both sides.
The surgeon tests the anastomosis by filling the pelvis with water and performing a sigmoidoscopy, confirming visually that there is no leak.

The surgeon places the remnant of the distal end into an endobag and extracts it through the lateral port. The video shows the port placement and extraction site, with the stoma closed by excising the colostomy area and appropriately closing the fascia.
The surgeon closes the skin and soft tissue layers, applying surgical glue to seal the incision site.
